title: Quiz: Gate Cancellation and Inverse Removal
description: Test your understanding of gate cancellation techniques and inverse gate identification

questions.question: Which of the following gates are self-inverse (applying twice gives identity)?

options.text: H, X, Y, Z, CNOT, and SWAP
Only the Pauli gates X, Y, Z
RX, RY, RZ with any angle

questions.explanation: H, X, Y, Z are single-qubit Hermitian gates (self-inverse). CNOT and SWAP are also self-inverse two-qubit gates. S and T are not self-inverse (S^2 = Z, T^4 = Z).

questions.question: What is the inverse of the T gate?
questions.explanation: The T gate applies a phase of pi/4. Its inverse Tdg (T-dagger) applies a phase of -pi/4. T is not self-inverse: T^2 = S, T^4 = Z, T^8 = I.

questions.question: RZ(0.5) followed by RZ(-0.5) on the same qubit results in:

options.text: Two separate RZ gates that cannot be simplified
A global phase that cannot be removed

questions.explanation: RZ(theta) and RZ(-theta) are inverses. Their composition is RZ(0) = I. Consecutive rotations about the same axis add their angles: 0.5 + (-0.5) = 0.

questions.question: Single-pass cancellation always finds all possible gate cancellations in a circuit.
questions.explanation: Single-pass cancellation only removes directly adjacent inverse pairs. After cancellation, previously separated gates may become adjacent, requiring additional passes. Iterative cancellation is needed for complete optimization.

questions.question: Consider the sequence: H-X-X-H on qubit 0. After iterative cancellation, how many gates remain?
questions.explanation: First pass: X-X cancels (both self-inverse), leaving H-H. Second pass: H-H cancels. All gates are eliminated through iterative cancellation.

questions.question: Why is commutation-aware cancellation more powerful than simple iterative cancellation?

options.text: It runs faster with fewer iterations
It can cancel gates that are not adjacent by reordering commuting operations
It only works on single-qubit gates
It preserves the original gate order better

questions.explanation: Commutation-aware cancellation can move gates past each other when they commute (act on different qubits or are diagonal in the same basis), enabling cancellation of inverse pairs that are separated in the original circuit.
